questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: 5.2.2 Attainment and Characteristics of Chemical Equilibria
questions.question: At equilibrium for the reaction \(\mathrm{N_2O_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NO_2(g)}\), which of the following statements is true about the concentrations of \(\mathrm{N_2O_4}\) and \(\mathrm{NO_2}\)?

options.B: They are constant but not necessarily equal.
options.C: They are decreasing at the same rate.

questions.hint: Equilibrium means no net change, not equal concentrations.
questions.explanation: At equilibrium, the concentrations of reactants and products become constant because the forward and reverse rates are equal. They are not necessarily equal to each other. Option B is correct. Option A is a common misconception.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 5.2.2 Attainment and Characteristics of Chemical Equilibria
source_trace.content_anchor: concentrations of reactants and products are not necessarily equal at equilibrium

explanations_tiered.definition: At equilibrium, concentrations of reactants and products are constant over time but are generally not equal to each other.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„ â‡Œ 2NOâ‚‚, the equilibrium constant Kc = [NOâ‚‚]آ²/[Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„] is constant at fixed T, but [NOâ‚‚] and [Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„] themselves are not equal unless Kc = 1 and specific initial conditions.
explanations_tiered.beginner: At equilibrium, the amounts stop changing, but that doesn't mean they're the same amount. You could have more brown gas (NOâ‚‚) or more colorless gas (Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Dynamic equilibrium means the rates are equal, so no net change. The actual concentrations of Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„ and NOâ‚‚ are constant but their values depend on Kc and initial conditions; they are equal only in special cases.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The equilibrium constant expression shows that [NOâ‚‚]آ² is proportional to [Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„]. Unless Kc = 1 and the stoichiometry allows, the concentrations are not equal. For Kc = 4, if [Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„] = 1 M, then [NOâ‚‚] = 2 M, not equal.
reasoning_steps: Recall that equilibrium means no net change in concentrations.
Recognize that constant does not mean equal.
The ratio [NOâ‚‚]آ²/[Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„] is constant, not the individual concentrations.
misconceptions: A very common misconception is that at equilibrium, [reactants] = [products].
Another error: thinking constant concentrations mean the reaction has stopped.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: At equilibrium: concentrations constant, not necessarily equal.

glossary_entry.definition: At equilibrium, concentrations are constant but not equal unless K = 1 and specific conditions hold.
glossary_entry.example_equation: For Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„ â‡Œ 2NOâ‚‚, [NOâ‚‚] and [Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„] are constant but not equal.

flashcard.front: At equilibrium for Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„ â‡Œ 2NOâ‚‚, are [Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„] and [NOâ‚‚] equal?
flashcard.flashcard_back: No. They are constant but not necessarily equal. Only the rates are equal.

explanations_tiered.workbook_section: 5.2.2 Attainment and Characteristics of Chemical Equilibria
explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Concentrations are constant at equilibrium but are not necessarily equal; only the rates are equal.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.sub_subsection: B. Equilibrium constant expression involving concentration, Kc
questions.question: Write the \(K_c\) expression for the reaction \(\mathrm{4NH_3(g) + 5O_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 4NO(g) + 6H_2O(g)}\).
questions.hint: Products in numerator, reactants in denominator, each raised to its coefficient.
questions.explanation: The Kc expression places product concentrations in the numerator and reactant concentrations in the denominator, each raised to the power of its stoichiometric coefficient. Option A is correct. Option C is the inverse (1/Kc).

source_trace.structural_element: B. Equilibrium constant expression involving concentration, Kc

explanations_tiered.definition: Kc is the ratio of product concentrations to reactant concentrations at equilibrium, each raised to the power of its stoichiometric coefficient.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For 4NHâ‚ƒ + 5Oâ‚‚ â‡Œ 4NO + 6Hâ‚‚O, all species are gases. Kc = [NO]âپ´[Hâ‚‚O]âپ¶ / ([NHâ‚ƒ]âپ´[Oâ‚‚]âپµ).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Put products (NO and Hâ‚‚O) on top, reactants (NHâ‚ƒ and Oâ‚‚) on bottom. Use the big numbers as exponents.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The coefficients become exponents: NO coefficient 4 â†’ [NO]âپ´, Hâ‚‚O coefficient 6 â†’ [Hâ‚‚O]âپ¶, NHâ‚ƒ coefficient 4 â†’ [NHâ‚ƒ]âپ´, Oâ‚‚ coefficient 5 â†’ [Oâ‚‚]âپµ. Products numerator, reactants denominator.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This is a homogeneous gas-phase reaction. The equilibrium constant expression is derived from the law of mass action: Kc = (a_NOâپ´)(a_H2Oâپ¶)/(a_NH3âپ´)(a_O2âپµ), where activities approximate concentrations for ideal gases.
reasoning_steps: Identify products: NO and Hâ‚‚O. Identify reactants: NHâ‚ƒ and Oâ‚‚.
Write products in numerator, reactants in denominator.
Raise each concentration to its coefficient from balanced equation.
misconceptions: Students often invert the expression (put reactants on top).
Another error: forgetting to raise to powers or using the wrong exponents.
explanations_tiered.workbook_section: 5.2.4 B. Equilibrium constant expression involving concentration, Kc
explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Products (NO, Hâ‚‚O) in numerator, reactants (NHâ‚ƒ, Oâ‚‚) in denominator, each raised to its coefficient.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.sub_subsection: C. Equilibrium constant expression involving partial pressure, Kp
questions.question: What is the \(K_p\) expression for the heterogeneous reaction \(\mathrm{CaCO_3(s) \rightleftharpoons CaO(s) + CO_2(g)}\)?
questions.hint: Pure solids are omitted from Kp expressions.
questions.explanation: Pure solids (CaCOâ‚ƒ and CaO) have constant activity (taken as 1) and are omitted from Kp. Only gases appear. Thus Kp = P_COâ‚‚. Option B is correct. Option A is equivalent but not the standard simplified form.

source_trace.structural_element: D. Equilibrium constant for heterogeneous reactions
source_trace.content_anchor: Kp = P_CO2 for CaCO3(s) â‡Œ CaO(s) + CO2(g)

explanations_tiered.definition: For heterogeneous reactions involving pure solids, the solids are omitted from the Kp expression; only gases appear.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: CaCOâ‚ƒ(s) and CaO(s) are pure solids with constant activity (1), so Kp depends only on the partial pressure of COâ‚‚.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Solids like CaCOâ‚ƒ and CaO don't appear in Kp. Only the gas (COâ‚‚) matters: Kp = P_COâ‚‚.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The activity of a pure solid is 1, so they are not written in the equilibrium constant expression. Thus Kp = P_COâ‚‚ for the decomposition of calcium carbonate.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Thermodynamically, Kp = exp(-خ”Gآ°/RT) = (a_CaO أ— a_COâ‚‚) / a_CaCOâ‚ƒ. Since a_CaO = a_CaCOâ‚ƒ = 1 for pure solids, Kp = a_COâ‚‚ = P_COâ‚‚/Pآ° (if using standard state). Often written as Kp = P_COâ‚‚ when pressure is in atm.
reasoning_steps: Identify physical states: CaCOâ‚ƒ(s), CaO(s), COâ‚‚(g).
Recall: pure solids are omitted from Kp.

variables.meaning: Partial pressure of COâ‚‚ at equilibrium

misconceptions: Some incorrectly include solids in Kp (Option C).
Others think Kp = 1/P_COâ‚‚ (inverse).

glossary_entry.definition: Kp = P_COâ‚‚ because CaCOâ‚ƒ and CaO are pure solids omitted from the expression.

flashcard.front: Write Kp for CaCOâ‚ƒ(s) â‡Œ CaO(s) + COâ‚‚(g).

explanations_tiered.workbook_section: 5.2.4 D. Equilibrium constant for heterogeneous reactions
explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Pure solids CaCOâ‚ƒ and CaO are omitted, so Kp = P_COâ‚‚.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.structural_anchor: ICE table with Kc and quadratic
questions.question: For \(\mathrm{H_2(g) + I_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2HI(g)}\), \(K_c = 49.7\) at 458آ°C. Initially, 0.100 mol Hâ‚‚ and 0.100 mol Iâ‚‚ are placed in a 1.00 L vessel. If the change in [Hâ‚‚] is x, which equation must be solved to find x?

source_trace.content_anchor: ICE table example with initial concentrations and Kc

explanations_tiered.definition: An ICE table with initial concentrations equal allows taking the square root to simplify solving for x.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Because initial [Hâ‚‚] = [Iâ‚‚], the Kc equation simplifies to (2x)آ²/(0.100 - x)آ² = 49.7, so taking square roots gives 2x/(0.100 - x) = âˆڑ49.7 = 7.05, which is linear in x.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Start with 0.100 M Hâ‚‚ and Iâ‚‚. If x reacts, you get 0.100 - x of each reactant and 2x of HI. Plug into K = (2x)آ²/(0.100 - x)آ² = 49.7.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: ICE: Hâ‚‚: 0.100 - x, Iâ‚‚: 0.100 - x, HI: 2x. Substituting into Kc = [HI]آ²/([Hâ‚‚][Iâ‚‚]) gives (2x)آ²/[(0.100 - x)(0.100 - x)] = (2x)آ²/(0.100 - x)آ² = 49.7.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Because the denominator is a perfect square and the numerator is (2x)آ², we can take the square root of both sides: 2x/(0.100 - x) = âˆڑ49.7 = 7.05. This avoids solving a quadratic equation. Solving gives x = 0.100أ—7.05/(2+7.05) = 0.078 M.
worked_solution: Step 1: ICE table: Hâ‚‚: 0.100 - x, Iâ‚‚: 0.100 - x, HI: 2x
reasoning_steps: Write equilibrium concentrations in terms of x.

formula_analysis.name: Kc equation for H2 + I2 â‡Œ 2HI with equal initial concentrations

misconceptions: Students often forget to square the 2 in (2x)آ² = 4xآ².
Another error: writing (0.100 - 2x) for Hâ‚‚ when coefficient is 1.
Some omit the square on the denominator.

glossary_entry.definition: For Hâ‚‚ + Iâ‚‚ â‡Œ 2HI with equal initial concentrations, Kc = (2x)آ²/([A]â‚€ - x)آ².

flashcard.front: For Hâ‚‚+Iâ‚‚â‡Œ2HI, K=49.7, initial [Hâ‚‚]=[Iâ‚‚]=0.100 M, write equation in x.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. ICE gives [Hâ‚‚]=[Iâ‚‚]=0.100-x, [HI]=2x, so K = (2x)آ²/(0.100-x)آ².

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.sub_subsection: A. Effect of Change in The Concentration
questions.question: For the reaction \(\mathrm{CH_4(g) + H_2O(g) \rightleftharpoons CO(g) + 3H_2(g)}\), what happens to the concentration of \(\mathrm{CO}\) when additional \(\mathrm{CH_4}\) is added at constant temperature and volume?
questions.hint: Adding a reactant shifts equilibrium toward products.
questions.explanation: Adding CHâ‚„ (a reactant) shifts the equilibrium to the right (toward products), increasing the concentrations of CO and Hâ‚‚. Option A is correct. Kc remains unchanged at constant temperature.

source_trace.structural_element: A. Effect of Change in The Concentration
source_trace.content_anchor: increase in concentration of reactant shifts equilibrium to right

explanations_tiered.definition: Adding a reactant increases its concentration, causing the equilibrium to shift toward products to consume the added reactant.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: CHâ‚„ is a reactant. Adding it disturbs equilibrium, making Q < K. The forward reaction is favored, producing more CO and Hâ‚‚, so [CO] increases.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Adding more CHâ‚„ gives the reaction more fuel to make CO, so CO goes up until a new equilibrium is reached.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Initially Q = [CO][Hâ‚‚]آ³/([CHâ‚„][Hâ‚‚O]). Adding CHâ‚„ increases denominator, making Q < K. The system responds by shifting right (forward), increasing [CO] until Q = K again.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Le Chatelier's principle: the stress (increased [CHâ‚„]) is counteracted by consuming CHâ‚„ via the forward reaction. This increases product concentrations, including CO. K is unchanged.
misconceptions: Some think adding a reactant always increases all product concentrations (true here).
Others believe K changes (false; only temperature changes K).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Add reactant â†’ shift right â†’ increase products.

glossary_entry.definition: Adding a reactant increases product concentrations at the new equilibrium.
glossary_entry.example_equation: Add CHâ‚„ to CHâ‚„ + Hâ‚‚O â‡Œ CO + 3Hâ‚‚ increases [CO].

flashcard.front: Adding CHâ‚„ to CHâ‚„+Hâ‚‚Oâ‡ŒCO+3Hâ‚‚ â€” does [CO] increase or decrease?
flashcard.flashcard_back: [CO] increases; equilibrium shifts right toward products.

explanations_tiered.workbook_section: 5.2.6 A. Effect of Change in The Concentration
explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Adding reactant CHâ‚„ shifts equilibrium to the right, increasing [CO].

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.sub_subsection: C. Effect of Change in Temperature
questions.question: For the endothermic reaction \(\mathrm{N_2O_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NO_2(g)}\), what happens to \(K_c\) when the temperature is decreased?
questions.hint: For endothermic reactions, decreasing temperature favors reactants, decreasing K.
questions.explanation: The reaction is endothermic (absorbs heat). Decreasing temperature removes heat, shifting equilibrium left (toward Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„). Therefore, Kc = [NOâ‚‚]آ²/[Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„] decreases. Option B is correct.

source_trace.structural_element: C. Effect of Change in Temperature
source_trace.content_anchor: decrease in temperature favors the exothermic direction, so for endothermic reaction, K decreases

explanations_tiered.definition: For an endothermic reaction (خ”H > 0), decreasing temperature decreases the equilibrium constant K.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Heat is a reactant for endothermic reactions. Decreasing temperature removes heat, shifting equilibrium left (toward reactants), so K = [NOâ‚‚]آ²/[Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„] decreases.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The reaction Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„ â†’ 2NOâ‚‚ needs heat. If you cool it down, less NOâ‚‚ forms, so K gets smaller.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„ â‡Œ 2NOâ‚‚ is endothermic (خ”H > 0). Lowering T favors the exothermic direction (reverse), reducing [NOâ‚‚] and increasing [Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„]. Thus Kc decreases.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The van 't Hoff equation shows d(ln K)/dT = خ”H/(RTآ²). Since خ”H > 0, K increases with T. Therefore, decreasing T decreases K.
reasoning_steps: System shifts to produce heat (exothermic direction = reverse).
Reverse direction favored means K decreases.
misconceptions: Some think decreasing temperature always decreases K (false for exothermic).
Others believe K is independent of temperature (false).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Endothermic: â†‘T â†’ â†‘K, â†“T â†’ â†“K.

glossary_entry.definition: For an endothermic reaction, increasing temperature increases K; decreasing temperature decreases K.

flashcard.front: For endothermic Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„ â‡Œ 2NOâ‚‚, does K increase or decrease when T is lowered?
flashcard.flashcard_back: K decreases; lower T favors reactants (Nâ‚‚Oâ‚„).

explanations_tiered.workbook_section: 5.2.6 C. Effect of Change in Temperature
explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Endothermic reaction: decreasing temperature shifts equilibrium left, decreasing Kc.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.sub_subsection: B. Effect of Change in Pressure
questions.question: In the Haber process, high pressure (200-400 atm) is used. A catalyst (iron) is also present. How does the catalyst affect the equilibrium response to pressure changes?

options.A: The catalyst magnifies the effect of pressure, shifting equilibrium further right.
options.B: The catalyst allows equilibrium to be reached faster but does not change the pressure effect.
options.C: The catalyst reduces the need for high pressure by increasing Kp.
options.D: The catalyst makes pressure changes irrelevant to equilibrium.

questions.hint: Catalysts affect only kinetics, not thermodynamics or equilibrium shifts.
questions.explanation: The iron catalyst speeds up both forward and reverse reactions equally, allowing equilibrium to be reached faster. It does not change K or the direction or magnitude of the pressure shift. Option B is correct.

source_trace.content_anchor: catalyst does not shift equilibrium position

explanations_tiered.definition: A catalyst increases the rates of both forward and reverse reactions equally, speeding attainment of equilibrium but not altering the equilibrium position or the effect of pressure changes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The iron catalyst in the Haber process lowers activation energy for both Nâ‚‚+3Hâ‚‚â†’2NHâ‚ƒ and 2NHâ‚ƒâ†’Nâ‚‚+3Hâ‚‚. It does not change K or the response to pressure (shift toward fewer gas moles).
explanations_tiered.beginner: The catalyst just makes the reaction reach equilibrium faster. It doesn't change whether high pressure helps make more ammonia â€” that's still true.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Catalysts do not affect thermodynamics. The equilibrium shift due to high pressure (خ”n = -2) is determined solely by خ”n and K. The catalyst only reduces the time needed to reach that equilibrium.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The equilibrium constant K is a thermodynamic quantity independent of path. A catalyst provides an alternative mechanism with lower Ea, increasing k_f and k_r equally, so K = k_f/k_r unchanged. Pressure effects on K are nonexistent (K independent of P for ideal gases), but pressure affects Q and thus the direction of shift â€” the catalyst does not alter this.
reasoning_steps: Recall catalyst does not change K or equilibrium position.
Pressure effect depends only on خ”n, not on catalyst.
Catalyst only affects rate, not equilibrium shift.
misconceptions: Some think catalyst increases yield (false).
Others believe catalyst changes how pressure affects equilibrium (false).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Catalyst: faster equilibrium, same pressure response.

glossary_entry.definition: A catalyst does not change how pressure affects equilibrium; it only speeds up reaching the new equilibrium.
glossary_entry.example_equation: Iron catalyst in Haber process: high pressure still favors NHâ‚ƒ, catalyst only accelerates.

flashcard.front: Does the iron catalyst in Haber process change how pressure affects NHâ‚ƒ yield?
flashcard.flashcard_back: No. Catalyst speeds up equilibrium attainment but does not change the pressure effect (high pressure still favors NHâ‚ƒ).

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The catalyst speeds up reaching equilibrium but does not change the direction or magnitude of the pressure shift.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.sub_subsection: A. Haber Process for the Manufacture of Ammonia
questions.question: What are the raw materials used in the Haber process for ammonia production?
questions.hint: Recall the balanced equation for ammonia synthesis.
questions.explanation: The Haber process synthesizes ammonia from nitrogen (Nâ‚‚) and hydrogen (Hâ‚‚) according to Nâ‚‚ + 3Hâ‚‚ â‡Œ 2NHâ‚ƒ. Option A is correct. Hydrogen is typically obtained from natural gas reforming, but the direct reactants are Nâ‚‚ and Hâ‚‚.

source_trace.structural_element: A. Haber Process for the Manufacture of Ammonia

explanations_tiered.definition: The Haber process combines nitrogen and hydrogen gases to produce ammonia.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Raw materials: Nâ‚‚ (from air) and Hâ‚‚ (from natural gas reforming or other sources). The reaction is Nâ‚‚ + 3Hâ‚‚ â‡Œ 2NHâ‚ƒ.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Ammonia is made from nitrogen (from air) and hydrogen (from natural gas or water).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The Haber process uses Nâ‚‚ and Hâ‚‚ as direct reactants. Nâ‚‚ is obtained from air, Hâ‚‚ from steam reforming of methane or other hydrocarbons.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The source of Hâ‚‚ is typically via the water-gas shift reaction: CHâ‚„ + Hâ‚‚O â†’ CO + 3Hâ‚‚, followed by CO + Hâ‚‚O â†’ COâ‚‚ + Hâ‚‚. Nâ‚‚ is obtained by fractional distillation of liquid air or from air separation units.
reasoning_steps: Identify the reactants: Nâ‚‚ and Hâ‚‚.
misconceptions: Some think air (which contains Oâ‚‚) is used directly (Oâ‚‚ would poison catalyst).
Others believe ammonia is made from ammonium salts directly.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Haber raw materials: Nâ‚‚ (air) and Hâ‚‚ (natural gas/water).

glossary_entry.definition: Nitrogen (from air) and hydrogen (from methane steam reforming or electrolysis).

flashcard.front: What are the two raw materials for the Haber process?

explanations_tiered.workbook_section: 5.2.7 A. Haber Process for the Manufacture of Ammonia
explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. The Haber process uses Nâ‚‚ and Hâ‚‚ as reactants to form NHâ‚ƒ.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.sub_subsection: B. Contact Process for the Manufacture of Sulphuric acid
questions.question: Which catalyst is used in the Contact process to oxidize \(\mathrm{SO_2}\) to \(\mathrm{SO_3}\)?
questions.hint: Modern Contact process uses a vanadium-based catalyst.
questions.explanation: Vanadium(V) oxide (Vâ‚‚Oâ‚…) is the catalyst used in the Contact process for the oxidation of SOâ‚‚ to SOâ‚ƒ. Option B is correct. Platinum was used historically but is more expensive and easily poisoned.

source_trace.structural_element: B. Contact Process for the Manufacture of Sulphuric acid
source_trace.content_anchor: V2O5 catalyst for oxidation of SO2 to SO3

explanations_tiered.definition: Vanadium(V) oxide (Vâ‚‚Oâ‚…) is the catalyst used in the Contact process to accelerate the oxidation of SOâ‚‚ to SOâ‚ƒ.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The Contact process uses Vâ‚‚Oâ‚… supported on silica or other carriers to catalyze 2SOâ‚‚ + Oâ‚‚ â†’ 2SOâ‚ƒ at about 450آ°C.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The catalyst in the Contact process is Vâ‚‚Oâ‚… (vanadium pentoxide). It helps SOâ‚‚ react with Oâ‚‚ to make SOâ‚ƒ faster.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Vâ‚‚Oâ‚… provides an alternative reaction pathway with lower activation energy for the oxidation of SOâ‚‚. It is preferred over platinum because it is less expensive and more resistant to poisons.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The mechanism involves redox cycling of vanadium: Vâ‚‚Oâ‚… + SOâ‚‚ â†’ Vâ‚‚Oâ‚„ + SOâ‚ƒ, then Vâ‚‚Oâ‚„ + آ½Oâ‚‚ â†’ Vâ‚‚Oâ‚…. This explains why Vâ‚‚Oâ‚… is an effective catalyst for this exothermic gas-phase reaction.
reasoning_steps: Recall the Contact process equation: 2SOâ‚‚ + Oâ‚‚ â†’ 2SOâ‚ƒ.
Remember the catalyst is Vâ‚‚Oâ‚… (vanadium pentoxide).
misconceptions: Some think platinum is still used (historically true but replaced by Vâ‚‚Oâ‚…).
Others confuse with Haber process catalyst (iron).

glossary_entry.definition: The catalyst used in the Contact process to convert SOâ‚‚ to SOâ‚ƒ.

flashcard.front: What catalyst is used in the Contact process for SOâ‚‚ to SOâ‚ƒ?

explanations_tiered.workbook_section: 5.2.7 B. Contact Process for the Manufacture of Sulphuric acid
explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Vanadium(V) oxide (Vâ‚‚Oâ‚…) is the catalyst in the modern Contact process.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.sub_subsection: D. Equilibrium constant for heterogeneous reactions
questions.question: For the heterogeneous reaction \(\mathrm{CO_2(g) + H_2O(l) \rightleftharpoons H^+(aq) + HCO_3^-(aq)}\), what is the correct \(K_c\) expression?
questions.hint: Pure liquids are omitted from Kc expressions; gases and solutes remain.
questions.explanation: Hâ‚‚O(l) is a pure liquid, so its concentration is constant and omitted from Kc. COâ‚‚(g) appears as [COâ‚‚] (its concentration in solution), and Hâپ؛(aq) and HCOâ‚ƒâپ»(aq) appear as usual. Option B is correct.

source_trace.structural_element: D. Equilibrium constant for heterogeneous reactions
source_trace.content_anchor: pure liquids omitted from Kc expression

explanations_tiered.definition: In heterogeneous equilibria, pure liquids are omitted from the equilibrium constant expression because their concentration is constant.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Hâ‚‚O is a pure liquid solvent; its concentration does not change appreciably during the reaction. Thus it is omitted, giving Kc = [Hâپ؛][HCOâ‚ƒâپ»]/[COâ‚‚].
explanations_tiered.beginner: Water is a liquid, so we don't include it in the K expression. Only dissolved COâ‚‚ and the ions Hâپ؛ and HCOâ‚ƒâپ» appear.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Pure liquids have constant activity (â‰ˆ1 in dilute solutions). Therefore Kc = (a_Hâپ؛ أ— a_HCOâ‚ƒâپ») / (a_COâ‚‚ أ— a_Hâ‚‚O) simplifies to [Hâپ؛][HCOâ‚ƒâپ»]/[COâ‚‚] because a_Hâ‚‚O = 1.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In aqueous solution, the activity of water is approximately 1 for dilute solutions. The equilibrium constant for the hydration of COâ‚‚ and subsequent dissociation is K = [Hâپ؛][HCOâ‚ƒâپ»]/([COâ‚‚] آ· [Hâ‚‚O]) but [Hâ‚‚O] is constant and absorbed into K, giving the form shown.
reasoning_steps: Identify gas dissolved: COâ‚‚(g) (treated as concentration in solution).
misconceptions: Students often include Hâ‚‚O(l) in Kc expression (Option A).
Others omit COâ‚‚ incorrectly (Option D).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Heterogeneous with pure liquid: omit the liquid. Kc = [Hâپ؛][HCOâ‚ƒâپ»]/[COâ‚‚].

glossary_entry.definition: For COâ‚‚(g) + Hâ‚‚O(l) â‡Œ Hâپ؛(aq) + HCOâ‚ƒâپ»(aq), Kc = [Hâپ؛][HCOâ‚ƒâپ»]/[COâ‚‚] (Hâ‚‚O omitted).

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Hâ‚‚O(l) is a pure liquid and is omitted; COâ‚‚, Hâپ؛, and HCOâ‚ƒâپ» appear in the expression.
